item.title: Oil Could Spike to $200 If Hormuz Remains Shut, Fesharaki Warns
item.description: Oil could surge to $150 or $200 a barrel if the near-closure of the Strait of Hormuz persists over the next six to eight weeks because of the Iran war, according to energy-market consultancy FGE NexantECA.

item.title: Raspberry Pi Reports 25% Sales Rise Driven by US and China
item.description: Raspberry Pi Holdings Plc, a British maker of small, low-cost computers, reported a 25% sales rise in 2025 as demand for its products in the US and China outweighed the impacts of the ongoing memory chip shortage.

item.title: Unilever Nears Completion of Food Unit Sale to McCormick
item.description: Unilever Plc said talks to sell most of its food business to McCormick & Co. are advanced and a final deal could be announced later on Tuesday in an historic move that will transform both companies.

item.title: UK House Prices Post Fastest Rise in 15 Months, Nationwide Says
item.description: UK house prices unexpectedly surged in March, according to one of the country’s largest mortgage lenders, as demand appeared to hold up at the start of the Iran war.

item.title: Goldman Still Sees Gold at $5,400 By Year-End Despite Downturn
item.description: Goldman Sachs Group Inc. retained its bullish view on gold despite the recent selloff, forecasting renewed gains by the end of 2026.

item.title: Britons Enter Iran Energy Shock With High Savings Buffer
item.description: The amount put away by British households remained historically high at the end of 2025, suggesting consumers have a buffer to absorb any hit from a resurgence in inflation caused by the Iran war.

item.title: UK Economy Expanded 0.1% in the Fourth Quarter
Singapore Warns of Costlier Power Bills Due to Mideast Conflict
item.description: Singapore is set to see higher electricity prices in the months ahead as the US-Israeli war against Iran tightens global energy supplies, authorities warned.

item.title: Cape Town Seeks to Emulate India’s High-End Outsourcing Push
item.description: Cape Town wants to break into a global outsourcing industry dominated by India, but it will require overcoming a vast gap in scale and cost competitiveness.

item.title: Abu Dhabi’s 2PointZero Splashes Out $2.25 Billion in US Gas Deal
item.description: A company overseen by a prominent Abu Dhabi royal has agreed to buy a US gas infrastructure firm for $2.25 billion, the latest sign that Gulf firms are pressing ahead with overseas dealmaking even as the regional conflict enters its second month.

item.title: Just Three Firms Are Driving Europe’s €420 Billion Stock Rout
item.description: Three former market darlings have accounted for over half of the €420 billion ($481 billion) wiped off European stocks this quarter.

item.title: Japan, Indonesia Pledge Deeper Ties Amid Energy Concerns
item.description: Indonesian President Prabowo Subianto and Japanese Prime Minister Sanae Takaichi pledged deeper economic ties and cooperation on energy security as the two countries grapple with market instability brought on by the war in Iran.

item.title: Credit Risk in Asia Poised for Biggest Monthly Spike Since 2023
item.description: The cost to insure better-rated Asian debt against default is on course to increase in March by the most for any month since 2023, signaling growing concern about the economic impact of the Iran War on the region’s borrowers.

item.title: BlackRock to Launch Quant Fund Trading Singapore Stocks
item.description: BlackRock Inc. plans to launch a fund next month focused on large Southeast Asian stocks, according to people familiar with the matter, a move supporting Singapore’s drive to boost liquidity in the local market.

item.title: Australia to Profit from War Pushing Up Prices, Westpac Says
item.description: Australia will likely gain an unexpected multi-billion dollar windfall from the Iran war, as higher coal and gas export prices boost revenues over the five years to 2030, according to Westpac Banking Corp.

item.title: Japan Government Sets Up Task Force to Secure Medical Supplies
item.description: Japan’s economy and health ministries are establishing a joint task force to ensure that the country won’t run out of medicines and health products as the conflict in the Middle East disrupts supplies of critical goods.

item.title: Why Is the Japanese Yen So Weak - and Will Authorities Intervene?
item.description: The yen’s weakness has become a growing concern for Japan’s policymakers, given its impact on import prices and household costs.

item.title: India Delays Stricter Trading Loan Rules as Volatility Climbs
item.description: India’s central bank has delayed the roll-out of stricter rules on loans to proprietary traders and some liquidity providers, offering relief as markets are roiled by volatility due to the Iran conflict.

item.title: Japan Spot Power Surges to Three-Year High as War Boosts Fuels
item.description: Spot power prices in Japan surged to a three-year high, as the war in the Middle East boosts fuel costs for economies across Asia.

item.title: Australia Aims to Use LNG Clout to Secure Asian Fuel Supplies
item.description: Australia is seeking to leverage its vast liquefied natural gas exports to Asia to secure fuel supplies in return, as the country grapples with shortages triggered by the war in Iran.

item.title: Malaysian Chemical Producer Tops Asia Stocks With 102% Rally
item.description: Shares of Malaysian petrochemical producer Petronas Chemicals Group Bhd. have doubled this month as fears of a prolonged Strait of Hormuz closure drive up global fertilizer prices, with analysts predicting more gains if the waterway remains shut.

item.title: Why Bond Markets Are Changing Their Mind on the War
item.description: The limits to growth are starting to weigh more heavily that the risk of inflation.

item.title: Kosovo FDI May Top 2025’s Record €1 Billion, Central Banker Says
item.description: Kosovo is on its way to attracting a record amount of foreign direct investment after last year’s level exceeded €1 billion ($1.2 billion) for the first time in the country’s history, according to the central bank governor.

item.title: South Korea Rolls Out $17 Billion Budget to Counter Iran Shock
item.description: South Korea unveiled a supplementary budget worth billions of dollars to cushion its economy from the fallout of the Iran war, as soaring oil prices and global supply disruptions intensify pressure on households and businesses.

item.title: Japan’s Two-Year Bond Auction Draws Demand on Elevated Yields
item.description: Japan’s two-year government bond auction passed smoothly as the high yield lured investors despite caution over a potential near-term rate hike by the Bank of Japan.

item.title: BOK Governor Pick Plays Down FX Risks, Spurns Dove-Hawk Labels
item.description: Bank of Korea governor nominee Hyun Song Shin played down the importance of recent slides in the local currency and cautioned against simplistic labeling of policy as dovish or hawkish as he prepares for a confirmation hearing.

item.title: US Senators Probe FCC Chief Over Fast-Tracked Nexstar-Tegna Deal
item.description: The ranking members of the Senate Commerce Committee questioned the head of the Federal Communications Commission in a Monday letter, criticizing his approval of Nexstar Media Group Inc.’s merger with Tegna Inc. without a vote of the full commission.

item.title: Philippine Inflation May Accelerate to a 20-Month High on Iran War, BSP Says
item.description: The raging war in Iran that caused domestic fuel prices to spike probably pushed this month’s inflation in import-dependent Philippines to the highest in nearly two years, according to the central bank’s forecast.

item.title: Aluminum Heads for 10% Monthly Surge as Iran War Roils Supplies
item.description: Aluminum headed for the biggest monthly gain in nearly two years, as the war in the Middle East disrupted supplies and damaged local production facilities, tightening the global market.

item.title: Thai PM Anutin Gets Cabinet Approval, Keeps Core Economic Team
item.description: Thailand’s new cabinet has secured royal approval, with Prime Minister Anutin Charnvirakul retaining his core economic team as the country grapples with rising fuel costs, weakening exports and slowing growth.
